moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Polish Opening, an unconventional but playable first move that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside. By advancing the b-pawn, we control the c5 and a5 squares, preparing to support our pieces with a flexible pawn structure. This move catches many opponents off-guard because it violates typical opening principles—we're not controlling the center immediately—but it gives us time to develop a unique setup, often following up with Bb2 to activate our bishop on the long diagonal. While it's never been played at the highest levels in critical games, it's sound enough for intermediate players looking to avoid mainstream theory.

Black chooses an unusual developing move, placing the knight on the rim instead of the more natural f6 or c6. This move is playable but somewhat slow, as the knight on h6 doesn't control central squares and can be awkwardly placed. Black is likely preparing to reroute the knight via f5 or e5 depending on how we continue, or possibly eyeing g4 to provoke our pieces. The move shows that after 1. b4, Black hasn't found an immediate tactical blow, so they're taking time to develop—we've succeeded in making the position unusual, which can play to our advantage if we understand the resulting structure better than our opponent.
